These many sacrifices, which were all made unnecessary by the death of Christ, teach us that our best efforts need cleansing in his blood, and that the guilt of even our best sacrifices needs to be removed by someone more pure and more noble than they. Let us be thankful that we have such a High Priest. The priests had no breaks from service allowed. God’s spiritual priests have constant work, which daily duty requires; those who want to give an account with joy must use time wisely. The glory of God appeared in the sight of the people and acknowledged what they had done. We are not now to expect such appearances, but God draws near to those who draw near to him, and the offerings of faith are acceptable to him; though the sacrifices being spiritual, the signs of acceptance are spiritual as well. When Aaron had done all that was to be done about the sacrifices, he lifted up his hands towards the people and blessed them. Aaron could only ask for a blessing; God alone can command it.

When the ceremony was finished and the blessing pronounced, God testified his acceptance. A fire came out from before the Lord and consumed the sacrifice. This fire might justly have fallen upon the people and consumed them for their sins; but its consuming the sacrifice signified God’s acceptance of it as an atonement for the sinner. This also was a symbol of good things to come. The Spirit descended upon the apostles in fire. And the descent of this holy fire into our souls, to kindle in them pious and devout affections toward God, and such a holy zeal that burns up the flesh and its desires, is a certain sign of God’s gracious acceptance of our persons and actions. Nothing goes to God but what comes from him. We must have grace, that holy fire, from the God of grace, else we cannot serve him acceptably. The people were affected by this display of God's glory and grace. They received it with the highest joy, rejoicing in the assurance given them that they had God near to them. And with the lowest reverence; humbly worshipping the majesty of that God who deigned thus to manifest himself to them. That is a sinful fear of God which drives us from him; a gracious fear makes us bow before him.
